_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: As of June 2026, WTI crude oil is priced at approximately $69.6 per barrel, Brent crude at $69.56 per barrel, and US regular gasoline at $3.78 per gallon. Henry Hub natural gas stands at $3.29 per MMBtu. Indexed to 2019 levels (set at 100), natural gas has shown the greatest volatility among these fuels, spiking sharply in 2022 before retreating, whereas crude oil and gasoline prices have tended to move more closely together. This volatility in natural gas prices reflects supply and demand sensitivities distinct from oil and gasoline markets.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which measures the average cost per megawatt-hour over a plant's lifetime, shows that solar PV is now at $44/MWh, onshore wind at $33/MWh, offshore wind at $75/MWh, gas combined cycle (CCGT) at $76/MWh, and coal at $110/MWh globally. Solar PV and onshore wind have become more economical than new coal or gas power generation, reflecting their lower levelised costs. Notably, solar PV’s cost has decreased by about 90% since 2010, dropping from roughly $460/MWh to $44/MWh, signaling significant advances and affordability in renewable energy technologies.
notes.currency: Approximately 20% of global oil trade is estimated to be settled outside the US dollar, a share that is gradually increasing. While this is an estimate—there is no official figure—the US dollar still dominates oil transactions worldwide. This shift is mainly attributed to Russia's pivot toward Asian buyers who increasingly use yuan and rupee-based settlements for oil cargoes. Despite this drift, the dollar remains the prevailing currency in oil trade.
notes.now: Current energy market dynamics reflect a complex interplay between fossil fuel prices, renewable energy costs, and currency movements in trade. Natural gas’s price volatility contrasts with more stable oil and gasoline pricing, while renewables increasingly offer cost-effective alternatives in electricity generation through lower levelised costs. Simultaneously, shifts in currency use for oil payments indicate evolving trade patterns without displacing the US dollar’s dominant role.
